result:
It begins with a photograph.  
A seaside market in winter. A row of huts made from corrugated tin painted aquamarine, ribs showing like the flanks of great fish. The sky to the east is bruised, violet and axle grease. Dunes beyond the last hut are shifting, slow dunes you can only see move if you stare for an hour and blink once.  

In the foreground stands a woman whose name I do not know and yet remember from three dreams ago. She is peeling an orange with her thumbnail, rind curling away in a single green spiral. Behind her legs, at ankle level, two gulls fight over the cracked torque of a toy watch. Salt water keeps licking the metal raw.  

I have been carrying this photograph in the pocket of whatever coat I happen to wear, moving it from the drawer in Moab to the duffel in Matala, laundering it by mistake with a fistful of ship tickets that dissolved in the suds. The woman is frozen forever on the cusp of looking upward, lips parting to say something I was meant to hear but could not because the shutter closed too soon. All these years later the sentence hangs out of her mouth like a songbird caught in driftnet.

This is all we have really, a collage in transit—the little theaters our skulls erect, plug-in dramas run off the trickle voltage of nights where we cannot sleep. Cities, lovers, difficult verbs we promised ourselves to master: they drift into the room like submerged bells, ringing in a note pitched below the register of grief. You can still feel them in the chest cavity though. Not like pain, more like tide: drawing itself out, folding back in, never quite the same volume twice.  

When I was seven, I buried a marble in an avocado field and forgot to tell anyone where. For twenty-three calendar years I pictured it suspended in loam, aching under an ever-thickening diameter of roots. Babies were born, revolutions sparked and guttered, someone invented the internet. All the while my poor cat’s-eye stayed forgot, swallowing unexplained darkness. Then one March I flew home and met the new owner of my grandparents’ farm. The avocados had been replaced with dragon fruit trellises. The soil had been scraped clean a dozen centimetres deep, the marble presumably now a migrant in the belly of some banana-seller’s truck rattling toward a border town whose name contains three consecutive vowels. I felt cheated, as though adulthood had pilfered one last indulgence of childhood. Still, something lightened: the world had kept the promise of motion, a solitary piece of glass grinding its way toward the sea, schooling me in the one sacrament I can still call miraculous—displacement.

Most of us think memory is a filing cabinet—open the drawer, extract a label, hold it to the light. But a cabinet stands still—in one house, one hemisphere. That is the wrong geometry for minds that are nothing less than weather systems in sneakers. The cabinet is really a constellation that forgot it was one: starfish memories clinging to each other without knowing why, filaments spider-silk-thin, breaking and re-knotting when the breeze changes.  

I owe to this realisation something more sustaining than religion: the capacity to view my own contradictions as fractal rather than sin. In one mood I lecture friends about minimalism; in another I buy moth-filled Persian coats on tenth-hand websites. I have argued both for and against arranged marriage in the same wedding toast. A therapist once diagnosed me as “chronically porous,” which sounds flattering until you realise porosity invites leaks. But these leaks are the entire endeavour. We are not here to be consistent; we are trampolines for momentary hordes of plankton, glittering when the angle is right.  

The economist in me is suspicious of metaphor—she prefers curves and catastrophe theory. She insists I should at least monetise my wanderlust. So occasionally I write itineraries for people who have not yet learned how to be lost: a three-day circuit of Icelandic mechanic shops that smell of diesel and cardamom buns, the clandestine library under Odessa’s opera house where staff will feed you tea the colour of church windows if you ask absently for books in extinct dialects. I charge minimally; the profit is entrance into strangers’ days. One client mailed back a single gold earring plucked from a Romanian flea market. I wear it until the clasp breaks, then bury it unceremoniously near a bus stop in Tbilisi. The cycle restarts, punk Sisyphus with better fashion sense.  

In the sleep lab where I once volunteered, electrodes mapped my cortical storms across a screen the width of two accomplices hip-to-hip. I watched from a plastic cot as green polygons pulsed, a proxy music for whatever spilled song lived underneath language. During REM bursts the polysomnographic trace resembled an aerial view of river deltas seen at dusk—frayed, iridescent—whereas slow-wave sleep was a buoy metronome off a fog coast.  

At 3:11 a.m. I dreamt of the photograph again, except now the woman had finished peeling the orange and tossed its moon segments into the wind. The gulls inhaled them mid-flight, acquiring human pupils. I woke gasping; the technician swore the EEG showed no anomaly. I did not tell her that what startled me was not gulls with eyes but the instantaneous translation of citrus into recognition: the fruit transforming bird into witness, as if the world’s sole need of us is to be audience for its astonishment.

Some nights I stage something less scientific. I dim all lights, perform an experiment in lyrical astronomy: mapping the pattern of ceiling-shadows the way of moonlight carve across the wall. There is a chance the shadows are yesterday traumas disguised in evening-wear; there is also a chance they are tomorrow’s mercies wearing yesterday’s suits halfway through their alterations. Either way, I like giving them bespoke measurements. I once measured the distance between two concurrent shadows at 0.8 cubits, then walked to a bar and sold that measurement—written on a sugar packet—for two cigarettes and a joke about Platonic solids.  

A writer I admired committed suicide the year we both turned thirty-four. At his memorial someone read a line he wrote: “The opposite of distance is not arrival but echo.” The room ached like a struck bell. Later, in an airport restroom, I found myself mouthing the words into a scratched mirror and realised I was answering his sentence with a picture instead of another sentence: the photograph again, of course, seaside tin huts, orange peel, watch without hands, unsent words blooding into fog. Echo replying to echo.

How do you finish something that is committed only to beginning? The woman in the photograph will never fully look up; the marble will orbit plantations I will never see; my skull will continue weather-reporting, archiving summers in my joints. There is a stage after catharsis still commonly mistaken for silence: it is the sound fruit makes when invisibly ripening, the hush inside a tide reversing without complaint. I mean this as comfort more than ornament.  

One day even forgetting itself may grow legs and walk toward the inventory it used to forbid. I have already drafted my parting talking points:

1. Hold contradictions lightly; they are wind passengers not tenants.  
2. Give the smallest objects permission to migrate.  
3. When unsure whether to speak or stay silent, plant a tree without announcement. By the time the leaves reach window-height you won’t remember who was supposed to be talking.  
4. Look up often enough that the sky remembers your pupils exist. A retina and a storm front can sign treaties not yet named by policy institutes.  
5. At least once, chase your photograph until the shutter forgets to close—instead of capturing it, be the light it mistakes for air.

I suspect this list suffices for any remaining miles, provided there are at least two socks in my bag and an unread poem scribbled on the underside of a museum ticket. Everything else—career, passport pages, even grief’s mileage—is negotiable tender. The world has never wanted us whole; it wants us porous, leaking possibility in all directions, sphere-shaped scholars of departure.  

So if ever the boats let me, I will ride whichever current ferries loose shoes and ghost currency to the same delta where oranges split open into wings. There I’ll plant an avocado seed with a cat’s-eye fused inside like a pupil. Perhaps decades later, a child passing by will kick the fallen fruit, hear the rattle, excavate, and find the marble shinier than when I last touched it. She’ll pocket it without recognition. And just like that, I will have completed my residency on earth—a sigh looping outward, traveling in secret through plumage, pulp, and strangers’ pockets, finally arriving nowhere but here, unzipped wide as sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
